title: 10 best herbal teas for high cholesterol
intro_desc: Herbal teas for high cholesterol are caffeine-free beverages made from various plants, flowers, and spices, which can help manage high cholesterol levels. These teas often contain beneficial compounds that inhibit cholesterol absorption, increase bile excretion, and promote overall heart health. Some popular herbal teas for high cholesterol include hawthorn, holy basil, and black or green tea. For example, hawthorn tea is rich in antioxidants that help reduce LDL (bad) cholesterol, while holy basil tea has anti-inflammatory properties that may lower triglycerides and cholesterol levels. Regularly consuming these teas can contribute to improved heart health, reduced risk of cardiovascular diseases, and an overall enhanced quality of life.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: White tea teas helps with high cholesterol because of its unique combination of antioxidants, flavonoids, and catechins. These compounds have been shown to help lower LDL (bad) cholesterol levels by inhibiting the absorption of dietary cholesterol and triglycerides in the gut. Additionally, white tea's antioxidant properties may also help reduce inflammation in blood vessels, which can contribute to the development of atherosclerosis, or hardening of the arteries, a major risk factor for high cholesterol.
remedy_properties: Antioxidants: Herbal white tea is rich in antioxidants, which help to neutralize free radicals that can damage blood vessels and contribute to high cholesterol.

Flavonoids: Herbal white tea contains flavonoids, such as quercetin, kaempferol, and catechins, which have been shown to improve lipid profiles by reducing LDL (bad) cholesterol levels and increasing HDL (good) cholesterol levels.

Polyphenols: Polyphenols in herbal white tea have been found to inhibit the production of angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE), a key enzyme involved in blood pressure regulation, which can help to reduce the risk of high blood pressure, a major contributor to high cholesterol.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and flavonoids, the roots of the white tea plant help reduce LDL (bad) cholesterol levels and improve overall cardiovascular health.
Rhyzomes: Containing bioactive compounds like catechins and polyphenols, the rhyzomes of white tea plants have been traditionally used to lower triglyceride levels and prevent atherosclerosis.
remedy_recipe: Measure out one teaspoon of loose-leaf camellia sinensis per 8 ounce cup.
Heat filtered water to a temperature of 150-160°f and let it cool for 30 seconds.
Steep the tea leaves in the heated water for 2-3 minutes to allow for optimal extraction.
Strain the tea into a cup using a tea infuser or fine-mesh strainer to remove loose leaves.
Serve the herbal white tea hot and enjoy within 30 minutes of preparation.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Large-leaved lime teas helps with high cholesterol because it contains flavonoids and other bioactive compounds that have been shown to help reduce triglycerides and LDL ("bad") cholesterol levels. The tea's antioxidant properties also help protect against the oxidation of low-density lipoproteins, which can contribute to plaque buildup in arteries and increase the risk of heart disease. Additionally, herbal lime teas may help lower blood pressure and improve overall cardiovascular health.
remedy_properties: Hypolipidemic properties: Large-leaved lime tea has been shown to reduce triglycerides and LDL (bad) cholesterol levels, helping to alleviate symptoms of high cholesterol.

Antioxidant activity: Large-leaved lime tea contains antioxidants like vitamin C, beta-carotene, and other polyphenols that help neutralize free radicals and reduce oxidative stress, which can contribute to high cholesterol development.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The flavonoids and alkaloids present in large-leaved lime tea have been found to have anti-inflammatory effects, which can help reduce inflammation in blood vessels and alleviate symptoms of high cholesterol, such as chest pain and discomfort.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in flavonoids and saponins, which have been shown to help lower LDL cholesterol levels and improve overall cardiovascular health.
Bark: Contains triterpenoid saponins that have been traditionally used to treat various cardiovascular disorders, including hyperlipidemia.
remedy_recipe: Gather 2 tablespoons of fresh tilia platyphyllos leaves from the garden or forage them sustainably.
Rinse the gathered leaves with cold water to remove any dirt or debris gently.
Combine the rinsed leaves with 250ml of boiling water in a large glass infuser or teapot immediately.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes, allowing the flavonoids and tannins to infuse slowly.
Strain the tea into cups using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth, discarding the solids finally.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Maidenhair tree teas helps with high cholesterol because its unique combination of flavonoids, saponins, and antioxidants works to improve blood lipid profiles. The tea's flavonoids help to increase the production of liver receptors that bind to low-density lipoprotein (LDL) "bad" cholesterol, promoting its excretion from the body. Meanwhile, the saponins in the tea inhibit the absorption of dietary cholesterol and help to lower LDL levels.
remedy_properties: Antioxidant activity: The leaves of Maidenhair fern contain antioxidants like flavonoids and phenolic acids, which can help combat oxidative stress and inflammation, both of which are associated with high cholesterol.
Hypolipidemic effects: Adiantum extracts have been shown to reduce triglyceride levels and LDL ("bad") cholesterol in animal studies, potentially helping to lower overall cholesterol levels.
Anti-inflammatory properties: The fern's leaves contain anti-inflammatory compounds that may help alleviate inflammation in the blood vessels, which can contribute to high cholesterol.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in saponins and flavonoids, which help to lower LDL cholesterol levels and improve overall cardiovascular health.
Bark: Contains terpenoids that have been shown to inhibit the absorption of dietary cholesterol and reduce triglyceride levels.
remedy_recipe: Harvest one ounce of fresh or dried ginkgo biloba leaves.
Clean the harvested leaves thoroughly with distilled water.
Combine the cleaned leaves with one quart of boiling water in a large pot.
Steep the mixture for 10 to 15 minutes before straining and discarding solids.
Strain and pour the cooled tea into a cup or bottle for immediate consumption.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Cat's claw teas helps with high cholesterol because it is rich in antioxidants, flavonoids, and alkaloids that help to lower LDL (bad) cholesterol levels and improve overall cardiovascular health. The tea has been shown to inhibit the formation of plaque in arteries, which can lead to heart disease, and also helps to reduce blood pressure and inflammation, all of which are contributing factors to high cholesterol. Regular consumption of cat's claw teas may help to maintain healthy cholesterol levels and promote overall well-being.
remedy_properties: Anthocyanins: These powerful antioxidants have been shown to reduce LDL (bad) cholesterol levels by inhibiting the formation of fatty deposits in the bloodstream, thereby preventing cardiovascular disease.

Flavonoids: Flavonoids present in cat's claw tea have been found to improve blood vessel function and lower blood pressure, both of which can help alleviate symptoms associated with high cholesterol, such as atherosclerosis and cardiac arrhythmias.
Quinovic acid glycosides: These compounds have been shown to exhibit hypocholesterolemic effects by inhibiting the production of cholesterol in the liver and increasing its elimination from the body.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the cat's claw plant are rich in antioxidants and flavonoids, which help to reduce inflammation and improve blood lipid profiles, making them a popular choice for teas aimed at lowering high cholesterol.

Bark: The bark of the cat's claw plant contains triterpenoid saponins, which have been shown to decrease LDL ("bad") cholesterol levels and increase HDL ("good") cholesterol levels, making it an effective ingredient in medicinal teas.
remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality dried uncaria tomentosa roots and stems in amounts suitable for desired tea strength.
Combine 1 tablespoon of dried cat's claw with 8 ounces of boiling water in a heat-resistant cup or teapot.
Steep the mixture for 5-10 minutes to allow the herbal properties to infuse into the water.
Strain the liquid through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove loose herbs and sediment.
Serve the cat's claw tea hot, allowing it to cool slightly before consuming within 24 hours.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Garlic teas helps with high cholesterol because of its unique compounds, such as allicin and ajoene, which have been shown to lower LDL (bad) cholesterol levels while raising HDL (good) cholesterol. These compounds work by inhibiting the production of cholesterol in the liver, reducing triglycerides, and improving blood vessel function. Additionally, garlic teas have antioxidant properties that help protect against oxidative stress, further contributing to its cholesterol-lowering effects.
remedy_properties: Allicin: This sulfur compound in garlic has been shown to inhibit the formation of cholesterol in the liver, reducing overall cholesterol levels and LDL ("bad") cholesterol.

Quercetin: This flavonoid antioxidant in garlic tea helps to reduce inflammation and oxidative stress, which can contribute to high cholesterol by improving blood vessel function and lowering triglycerides.
Garlic saponins: These compounds have been shown to decrease the absorption of dietary cholesterol and increase the excretion of bile acids, leading to a reduction in LDL cholesterol levels.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and bioactive compounds, garlic roots are valued for their ability to help lower LDL ("bad") cholesterol levels.
Rhyzomes: Containing a unique combination of sulfur-containing compounds, garlic rhyzomes have been traditionally used to help reduce blood lipid levels and improve overall cardiovascular health.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh garlic bulbs from your garden or store them properly.
Prepare the garlic by chopping it finely into small pieces.
Combine chopped garlic with dried herbs in a heat-resistant container.
Steep the mixture in boiling water for the recommended time.
Strain and serve hot as a soothing herbal tea.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Cinnamon teas helps with high cholesterol because it has been shown to reduce LDL ("bad") cholesterol levels by improving insulin sensitivity and glucose metabolism. The polyphenolic compounds in cinnamon, such as cinnamaldehyde, have been found to inhibit the formation of cholesterol in the liver and increase the excretion of bile acids, which can help lower total cholesterol levels. Additionally, cinnamon's anti-inflammatory properties may also contribute to its beneficial effects on cardiovascular health by reducing inflammation and oxidative stress.
remedy_properties: Antioxidant activity: Cinnamon's antioxidant properties help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation, which can contribute to high cholesterol levels by reducing LDL ("bad") cholesterol oxidation and improving overall cardiovascular health.

Lipid-lowering compounds: Cinnamon contains polyphenols like cinnamaldehyde, which have been shown to reduce triglycerides and LDL cholesterol levels by inhibiting the production of enzymes involved in cholesterol synthesis and absorption.

Insulin-sensitizing properties: Cinnamon's ability to improve insulin sensitivity helps regulate blood sugar levels, which is important for managing high cholesterol as insulin resistance can contribute to elevated triglyceride and LDL cholesterol levels.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and bioactive compounds, roots have been traditionally used to help reduce triglycerides and LDL cholesterol levels.

Bark: Containing cinnamaldehyde, a compound with anti-inflammatory properties, bark is often used to prevent the formation of plaque in arteries, reducing the risk of high cholesterol-related heart disease.
remedy_recipe: Harvest cinnamomum verum leaves from mature plants, carefully selecting fresh and aromatic leaves for use.
Rinse the harvested leaves with pure water to remove dirt and debris, ensuring cleanliness for infusion.
Measure out one teaspoon of dried cinnamomum verum bark or four teaspoons of fresh leaf material per 8 ounce cup of boiling water.
Steep the measured herbs in boiling water for 5-7 minutes, allowing flavors and essential oils to infuse and meld.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh strainer or cheesecloth, discarding solids and serving hot with honey or milk if desired.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Turmeric teas helps with high cholesterol because of its unique compound curcumin, which has potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. Curcumin has been shown to lower LDL (bad) cholesterol levels by inhibiting the formation of new cholesterol in the liver and improving the body's ability to remove excess cholesterol from the bloodstream. Additionally, turmeric tea may also help to increase HDL (good) cholesterol levels, further supporting heart health by reducing the risk of cardiovascular disease.
remedy_properties: Curcumin: Turmeric's active compound has potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help reduce inflammation in the bloodstream and improve blood lipid profiles by lowering LDL ("bad") cholesterol levels.

Alpha-linolenic acid (ALA): The omega-3 fatty acid present in turmeric tea has been shown to improve triglyceride levels and lower total cholesterol levels by reducing the absorption of dietary cholesterol into the bloodstream.

Ferulic acid: This phenolic compound found in turmeric tea has antioxidant properties that can help protect against oxidative stress, inflammation, and cardiovascular damage, which are all associated with high cholesterol.
remedy_parts: Rhyzomes: The rhyzome, or underground stem, is a rich source of curcuminoids, which have been shown to help reduce LDL cholesterol levels and improve overall cardiovascular health.
Roots: Turmeric roots contain a compound called volatile oil, which has been found to have anti-inflammatory properties that can help alleviate symptoms of high cholesterol.
remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried turmeric root or two teaspoons of fresh turmeric root.
Add one cup of boiling water to the turmeric root in a teapot or mug.
Steep for five to seven minutes to allow the flavors and antioxidants to infuse.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh strainer or cheesecloth if desired.
Drink immediately and enjoy the warm, spicy flavor with a comforting aroma.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Licorice teas helps with high cholesterol because it contains a compound called glycyrrhizin, which has been shown to help lower LDL (bad) cholesterol levels. The tea also contains flavonoids and saponins, which have antioxidant properties that help protect against oxidative stress and inflammation in the body. By reducing inflammation and improving lipid profiles, licorice teas may help to alleviate high cholesterol symptoms and promote overall heart health.
remedy_properties: Flavonoids: Licorice root contains flavonoids, which have been shown to inhibit the absorption of dietary cholesterol and reduce LDL (bad) cholesterol levels by increasing bile production.

Glycyrrhizin: The main compound in licorice root, glycyrrhizin, has been found to decrease triglycerides and LDL cholesterol while increasing HDL (good) cholesterol by inhibiting the conversion of cortisol into cortisone.

Saponins: Licorice tea contains saponins, which are known for their ability to emulsify and solubilize fat molecules, helping to remove excess cholesterol from the bloodstream and reduce its accumulation in the arteries.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the licorice plant contain glycyrrhizin, a compound that has been shown to help lower triglycerides and improve lipid profiles.
Bark: The bark of the licorice plant contains flavonoids and terpenoids, which have anti-inflammatory properties that may help reduce cholesterol levels.
remedy_recipe: Measure out one teaspoon of dried glycyrrhiza glabra root per eight ounces water.
Heat the measured water in a teapot or kettle until it reaches boiling point.
Add the measured dried glycyrrhiza glabra root to the hot water and allow steeping for seven minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or tea strainer, discarding the solids.
Serve the licorice tea warm or at room temperature, allowing the flavor to mellow slightly before consumption.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Roselle teas helps with high cholesterol because of its rich antioxidant properties, which help to reduce inflammation in the body. The tea's flavonoids and phenolic acids work to inhibit the production of low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol, also known as bad cholesterol, while increasing high-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol, or good cholesterol. Additionally, roselle teas have been shown to improve blood vessel function, allowing for better circulation and reduced strain on the cardiovascular system. This natural combination of benefits makes roselle tea a promising addition to a healthy lifestyle for managing high cholesterol.
remedy_properties: Hibiscus flavonoids: Hibiscus flavonoids have been shown to lower LDL (bad) cholesterol levels by inhibiting the absorption of dietary cholesterol and reducing triglycerides, thereby improving overall lipid profiles.

Vitamins and minerals: Roselle tea is rich in vitamins C, E, and B-complex, as well as minerals like calcium, magnesium, and potassium, which can help to lower cholesterol levels by reducing inflammation and improving cardiovascular health.

Phytochemicals: The phytochemicals present in Roselle tea, such as anthocyanins and saponins, have been found to possess antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties that can help to reduce the risk of heart disease by inhibiting the formation of blood clots and improving lipid metabolism.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are a popular choice for making medicinal teas due to their rich content of saponins, which have been shown to help reduce LDL cholesterol levels.
Rhyzomes: The rhyzomes contain bioactive compounds like flavonoids and phenolic acids, which can help lower triglycerides and improve overall cardiovascular health.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried calyces of hibiscus sabdariffa in amounts of one cup for every two cups of water required.
Rinse the calyces with cold running water and pat dry with a paper towel to remove any dirt or debris.
Combine one part of the calyces with two parts of boiling water in a large saucepan and let it steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a large pot or teapot, pressing on the solids to extract as much liquid as possible.
Allow the tea to cool slightly before serving hot or chilling in the refrigerator for at least two hours and up to 24 hours to allow flavors to meld.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Lemon balm teas helps with high cholesterol because of its unique ability to help reduce triglycerides and LDL (bad) cholesterol levels in the body. The tea's active compounds, such as citral and rosmarinic acid, work to improve blood flow and reduce inflammation, which can contribute to plaque buildup in arteries. Additionally, lemon balm has been shown to inhibit the absorption of dietary cholesterol, making it a natural and effective complement to traditional high-cholesterol management treatments.
remedy_properties: Antioxidant properties: Lemon balm tea's antioxidants help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation, which can contribute to high cholesterol by protecting against damage to LDL ("bad") cholesterol particles.

Cholesterol-lowering compounds: Lemon balm contains flavonoids and terpenes that have been shown to inhibit the absorption of dietary cholesterol and reduce triglycerides, helping to lower overall cholesterol levels.

Stress-reducing properties: Lemon balm tea's calming effects can help alleviate stress and anxiety, which are common factors contributing to high cholesterol by reducing cortisol levels, a hormone that can increase blood pressure and contribute to high cholesterol.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Lemon balm leaves have been traditionally used to reduce inflammation and improve circulation, which can help lower cholesterol levels.
Roots: The roots of the lemon balm plant contain compounds that help to lower triglycerides and improve lipid metabolism, making it effective in reducing high cholesterol.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh melissa officinalis leaves or use dried leaves in bulk quantities of 2-3 teaspoons per cup.
Measure 1 cup of boiling water and pour it over the herbs in a heat-resistant teapot or infuser.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes to allow the flavonoids and essential oils to infuse into the liquid.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove any loose herb particles.
Allow the tea to cool slightly before serving, as lemon balm is best enjoyed at room temperature with minimal additives.

supplementary_best_treatment: The best way to treat high cholesterol with herbal teas is by incorporating specific types into your daily routine. Two effective options are holy basil and hibiscus teas. Holy basil, also known as tulsi, is known to lower LDL cholesterol levels and increase HDL cholesterol. Hibiscus tea, on the other hand, can help decrease both total cholesterol and LDL cholesterol levels. To fully harness their benefits, consume three cups of these teas daily, and maintain a balanced diet and regular exercise.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to high cholesterol that are treated with herbal teas include high blood pressure and poor digestion. For high blood pressure, hibiscus tea is often recommended, as it may help lower blood pressure in people with hypertension. For digestive issues, teas like ginger, peppermint, and chamomile can be beneficial. Ginger tea can reduce inflammation and improve digestion, while peppermint tea can soothe an upset stomach and reduce bloating. Chamomile tea has anti-inflammatory properties that can help with digestion and may also help lower blood pressure. However, it's important to consult with a healthcare provider before starting any new herbal remedies.
